symbols: Susanna bathing — innocence and purity violated by corrupt authority
the elders watching from behind — hidden sin and the abuse of judicial power
water — purity and cleansing, contrasted with the unclean intentions of the watchers
garden setting — the enclosed private space that should be safe but has been violated
description: A tall, narrow composition showing a young woman standing in water up to her knees, her body exposed and her hands positioned modestly. She has dark curly hair and a downward gaze. Behind her, partially hidden in a doorway or opening with a golden-yellow background, two dark male figures observe her from the shadows. A purple cloth lies to one side. The water ripples around her legs. The composition emphasizes the contrast between the woman's vulnerable nakedness and the dark, lurking presence of her observers.
theological_description: The story of Susanna and the Elders, found in the deuterocanonical additions to Daniel (Daniel 13 in the Septuagint/Vulgate), tells of a beautiful and God-fearing woman falsely accused of adultery by two corrupt elders who had secretly lusted after her. When Susanna refused their advances — 'I am hemmed in on every side. For if I do this, it is death for me; and if I do not, I will not escape your hands. I choose not to do it and to fall into your hands, rather than to sin in the sight of the Lord' — the elders publicly accused her. Susanna was condemned to death until the young Daniel intervened, cross-examining the elders separately and exposing their contradictory lies. The story powerfully illustrates themes found throughout Scripture: the vulnerability of the righteous before corrupt authority, the vindication of the innocent by God, and the principle that 'the Lord sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the Lord looks on the heart' (1 Samuel 16:7). Susanna's trust in God amid false accusation prefigures Christ's own silence before his accusers (Matthew 26:63).

scripture_refs.reason: The story of Susanna and the Elders from the deuterocanonical additions to Daniel
God sees the heart, not outward appearances — Susanna's true innocence known to God despite false accusation
Commit your way to the Lord and he will vindicate — Susanna's trust rewarded through Daniel's intervention
